title: Swarm control: the artificial swarm
subtitle: The most reliable swarm-control method for a single hive, using what the bees already understand.
excerpt: When a colony has made swarm cells and the impulse is running, removing the queen cells buys a week at most. The artificial swarm works with the colony's swarm instinct rather than against it, splitting the flying bees from the nurse bees across two boxes so neither half has enough population to swarm again.
sourceNotes:
National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024 edition), swarm control section. https://www.nationalbeeunit.com
British Beekeepers Association Bee Craft technical data sheets, swarm control and artificial swarm.

content.text: always return to the original hive site, whichever box they find there. So you move the brood and
to one side. Leave an empty box on the original site with the queen. The flying bees come home and behave as if they have swarmed. No bees are lost.

attrs.body: Act as soon as you find charged swarm cells (cells with larvae floating in royal jelly) during a routine inspection. If the cells are capped and the queen is nowhere on the frames, a swarm may have already left. The artificial swarm is most effective the moment you find the first charged cell, before the colony has made its decision.
attrs.title: When to do this

content.text: One empty hive body on a clean floor and roof. Enough drawn frames or fresh foundation to fill it. The floor can be a spare or a new one; it must be placed on the exact spot the original hive was standing.

Find the queen on the original frames. This is the most time-consuming part. Smoke lightly, work calmly, look at the frame faces rather than the cluster. The queen is longer than the workers, with a tapered abdomen and shorter wings relative to her body length. She will often be in the centre of the brood nest.

Once you have the queen, move the original hive body to one side, about a metre away. Set the empty hive body, with its new floor, on the original footprint. Put the frame the queen is on into the new box. Fill the new box with drawn frames or foundation to the same depth as the original brood box had. Replace the crown board and roof.
Move the original brood box, with the
, to the side position. Remove all but one or two of the swarm cells from the combs, leaving only the best-looking, most central cell. The
box (or the moved original box) will raise that cell into a new queen, which will mate and start laying within three to four weeks.

All flying bees will return to the original site and find the new box with the old queen. The old brood box, sitting to the side, gets only nurse bees staying with the brood, plus the emerging young bees over the following fortnight. It becomes a smaller, non-swarming unit that will be headed by the new queen once she has mated.

After seven days, inspect the moved box to check the remaining swarm cell is intact and that no new ones have been started. After three to four weeks, the new queen should be laying. At that point you can unite the two colonies with newspaper if you want a single strong hive, or keep them as two if you want to build hive numbers. The original queen's box will be laying well through the honey flow.
